g.data-narration: Welcome to File Handling in C++. Learn how to persist data using fstream classes for real-world applications.
g.text: File Handling in C++: fstream and File Classes

g.data-narration: File handling enables persistent data storage, allowing programs to save and retrieve data between executions.
g.text: Data Persistence: Files allow programs to store data permanently on disk
Unlike variables, file data survives program termination
Essential for applications: databases, logs, configuration files, records

g.data-narration: C++ provides three file stream classes: ifstream for reading, ofstream for writing, and fstream for both operations.
The file stream classes inherit from standard stream classes: ifstream from istream, ofstream from ostream, fstream from iostream.
File modes determine how files are opened: ios::in for reading, ios::out for writing, ios::app for appending, and ios::binary for binary data.
Files can be opened using constructor initialization: ofstream outFile(
g.text: ⚠ Default: ofstream opens in ios::out | ios::trunc (overwrites existing!)

g.data-narration: Always close files using close() to flush buffers and release system resources. Although destructors close automatically, explicit closing is best practice.
Text file operations use insertion (<<) and extraction (>>) operators, similar to cin and cout. Use getline() for entire lines.
File streams support all standard stream operations: operator<< for output, operator>> for input, and getline() for line-by-line reading.
Use seekg() and seekp() to move file pointers for random access. tellg() and tellp() return current pointer positions.
g.text: Example: file.seekg(10, ios::beg); // Move 10 bytes from start

g.data-narration: Binary files use read() and write() functions with reinterpret_cast to store raw data structures efficiently. Open with ios::binary mode.
g.text: Binary File Operations: read() and write()
⚠ reinterpret_cast<char*> converts address to char pointer for read/write operations

g.data-narration: Always check file operations using is_open(), fail(), or the extraction operator condition. Handle errors gracefully to prevent crashes.
g.text: ✓ Better: while(inFile >> variable) - stops automatically at EOF

g.data-narration: Remember: ifstream reads, ofstream writes, fstream does both. Always check is_open() and use binary mode for structures.
Avoid common mistakes: forgetting to check file opening, using wrong modes, confusing seekg/seekp, and not using binary mode for structures.
g.text: ① Not checking is_open() | ② Wrong mode (truncates!) | ③ Forgetting binary for structs
④ Confusing seekg() vs seekp() | ⑤ Missing reinterpret_cast in binary operations
